feed.title: Combined summary - Fountain Codes: a way to reduce blockchain storage costs

entry.title: Combined summary - Fountain Codes: a way to reduce blockchain storage costs
entry.summary: Fountain Codes are playing a pivotal role in enhancing the efficiency and scalability of the Bitcoin blockchain by reducing storage demands on individual nodes. Through this advanced coding technique, the blockchain is divided into numerous encoded segments called droplets, enabling nodes to reconstruct the entire blockchain with only a subset of these droplets. This approach significantly lessens the storage burden, as nodes do not need to retain the complete dataset. The collaboration between researchers and Vinteum has been instrumental in exploring these applications, aiming to optimize Bitcoin network operations. Further details on this research can be found in a comprehensive blog post at [this link](https://lucasdbr05.com/posts/fountain-codes/). The concept of deterministic droplet composition is based on per-peer seeds and block heights, which allows nodes to progressively build up their blockchain representation without needing to store all blocks immediately. This method involves a formulaic selection of droplets, ensuring both data savings and unbiased random selection among peers. However, the impact of this methodology in real-time scenarios seems to offer minimal immediate improvements to the current infrastructure but holds promise for future scalability and robustness enhancements. Moreover, there are considerations regarding the potentially increased load on node connections during the Initial Block Download (IBD) phase, necessitating strategies such as block-relay-only connections. The effectiveness of Fountain Codes must also consider factors like data serialization sizes and block content variability, which could affect the overall efficiency of the droplet-based storage system. Previous proposals have suggested using Reed-Solomon (RS) codes instead of Fountain Codes due to concerns over security against malicious peers and the risk of node fingerprinting. RS codes allow for polynomial-time decoding even in the presence of errors, offering a more secure alternative for data reconstruction when some peers may be unreliable. Additionally, the choice of a simpler coding scheme could address intellectual property concerns and aid in maintaining node anonymity over its operational lifetime, thus prioritizing privacy and security within the network’s protocol design decisions.
